items.question: Who uses stochastic gradient descent in practice?
items.answer: ML engineers, researchers, and framework developers use mini-batch SGD (or SGD-based optimizers) to train neural networks and large linear models whenever full-batch gradient descent is too slow or memory-intensive.

items.question: What is the SGD parameter update rule?
items.answer: θ ← θ − η ∇L_B(θ), where ∇L_B(θ) is the average gradient of the loss over a randomly sampled mini-batch B and η is the learning rate.

items.question: When should you prefer mini-batch SGD over full-batch gradient descent?
items.answer: When the training set is large enough that computing a full-batch gradient each step is impractical, and when many noisy updates per epoch yield faster wall-clock progress on GPU or CPU.

items.question: Where does randomness enter the SGD training loop?
items.answer: In the random shuffle or sampling of indices that form each mini-batch before forward pass, loss computation, backpropagation, and the parameter update.

items.question: Why is the mini-batch gradient an acceptable substitute for the full gradient?
items.answer: Under uniform random sampling, E[∇L_B(θ)] = ∇L(θ), so each step points in the correct direction on average; variance decreases as batch size increases.

items.question: How does batch size affect updates per epoch?
items.answer: With dataset size N and batch size B (ignoring remainder), one epoch performs about ⌈N/B⌉ gradient steps; smaller B means more updates and noisier gradients per epoch.

items.question: How does mini-batch SGD compare to full-batch gradient descent?
items.answer: Full-batch descent uses all N examples for one exact gradient and one update per epoch—stable but slow. Mini-batch SGD uses B ≪ N examples per update, giving N/B noisier but cheaper steps per epoch and enabling large-scale training.

items.question: How does pure SGD (batch size 1) compare to mini-batch SGD with B=64?
items.answer: Batch size 1 has the highest gradient variance and most updates per epoch; B=64 averages 64 gradients, reducing noise and often allowing a larger learning rate, but yields fewer updates per epoch for the same data pass.
